coredata.dc:description: Green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) provides an eco-friendly method for producing nanomaterials. However, key factors like biomass composition, severity index, solid loading, and reaction kinetics remain underexplored. This study analyzed rice husk composition to assess its constituents’ impact on CDs formation. An experimental design identified optimal biomass degradation at 220 °C and 3 % w/v solids loading. A kinetic study at these conditions varied reaction times from 0 to 12 h. Critical process parameters, such as total sugars (TS), quantum yield (QY), and degradation compounds (DC), were monitored to provide mechanistic insights into the formation of CDs from rice husks. The study revealed that the optimum synthesis condition was achieved at 220 °C, with 3 % w/v solid loading and 4 h of reaction. Furthermore, this work showed that macromolecules and extractives participate in the formation of carbon dots. Mainly, hemicellulose and cellulose monomers were identified, providing experimental evidence that the carbon dots, considering this macromolecule, involve chain breaking into small molecules, and these molecules, via ring-opening reactions, condensation, and finally the aromatization and carbonization form carbon dots. However, no lignin monomers were identified to support the proposed mechanism. The reduction of lignin content provides insight into the participation of this macromolecule in carbon dots formation. Unraveling the mechanism of CD formation represents a significant challenge. This pioneering work contributes substantially to a deeper understanding of CD formation from rice husks and encourages large-scale synthesis. R-CDs showed sensitivity in detecting the pesticide atrazine (LOD = 2.91 µmol/L).

head.ce:title: Uncovering engineering and mechanistic insights in green synthesis of carbon dots from rice husks

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) provides an eco-friendly method for producing nanomaterials. However, key factors like biomass composition, severity index, solid loading, and reaction kinetics remain underexplored. This study analyzed rice husk composition to assess its constituents’ impact on CDs formation. An experimental design identified optimal biomass degradation at 220 °C and 3 % w/v solids loading. A kinetic study at these conditions varied reaction times from 0 to 12 h. Critical process parameters, such as total sugars (TS), quantum yield (QY), and degradation compounds (DC), were monitored to provide mechanistic insights into the formation of CDs from rice husks. The study revealed that the optimum synthesis condition was achieved at 220 °C, with 3 % w/v solid loading and 4 h of reaction. Furthermore, this work showed that macromolecules and extractives participate in the formation of carbon dots. Mainly, hemicellulose and cellulose monomers were identified, providing experimental evidence that the carbon dots, considering this macromolecule, involve chain breaking into small molecules, and these molecules, via ring-opening reactions, condensation, and finally the aromatization and carbonization form carbon dots. However, no lignin monomers were identified to support the proposed mechanism. The reduction of lignin content provides insight into the participation of this macromolecule in carbon dots formation. Unraveling the mechanism of CD formation represents a significant challenge. This pioneering work contributes substantially to a deeper understanding of CD formation from rice husks and encourages large-scale synthesis. R-CDs showed sensitivity in detecting the pesticide atrazine (LOD = 2.91 µmol/L).

ce:keyword.ce:text: Carbon dots from rice husk biomass

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: By 2050, the global population will reach 9.7 billion, peaking at 10.4 billion in the 2080s. Rice is one of the essential staple foods that sustains the global population, with a worldwide production of 787.3 million tons in 2021 [1]. Rice husk (RH) is a by-product of rice manufacturing, mainly composed of silica, cellulose, and minerals, making up more than 20 % of the total weight of the grain [2]. After milling, millions of tons of RH (∼157.5 million tons based on 2021 data) are generated and incorrectly disposed of in the environment, aggravating environmental problems [3]. The environmental consequences of improper disposal and management of this waste are a global problem. RH is considered low-value-added agricultural waste, with a high ash content after burning, making it difficult to use as a fuel [4]. Utilizing waste and adding value is a great challenge [5–8]. Therefore, many current studies focus on transforming RH into value-added products [9–14].

CDs are value-added nanomaterials with promising applications in several fields [15–18]. These materials present unique properties and, above all, the ease with which they can be obtained since they are synthesized from any carbon source. Typically, CDs are considered nanometric carbon materials with a size of less than 10 nm and an almost spherical morphology [19]. Their characteristics include fluorescence properties, high chemical stability, versatility in surface modification, non-toxicity, and superior hydrophilicity [20]. These materials exhibit unique luminescent properties and can emit visible and ultraviolet light when excited by light or other energy sources. The spectrum of applications is vast, ranging from biological and chemical sensors, optoelectronic devices, markers for cell tracking and photodynamic therapy, ion detectors, and photocatalysts [18,21].

Several works obtained CDs from RH focused on various applications [3,22,23], such as the degradation of dyes [24], detection of metal ions [3,23,25,26], selective sensors for sensitive detection of Fe3+ and fluoroquinolones [22], and bioimaging [27], among others [28].

Despite advancements in the application of carbon derived from biomass (CDs) using organic waste (UR), there remains a significant gap in determining biomass composition and its relationship with the formation of CDs. The primary limitation in studies on the green synthesis of CDs is the difficulty in quantifying the constituents of raw biomass, the final product, and the solid byproduct, with analyses often restricted to operational parameters such as reaction time and temperature. Since the synthesis of CDs relies on the solubilization of organic matter and its transformation into degradation products, it is crucial to conduct a thorough assessment of the impact of these phenomena during hydrothermal synthesis. In this context, analyzing the influence of solid load, temperature, and reaction time on the final properties of the product can provide valuable insights for optimizing CD synthesis [3,24,29]. This analysis will help to better understand how these factors affect the formation, quality, and characteristics of the produced CDs, enabling more precise and efficient process control.

Despite advances in the application of CDs from RH, there is still a gap in determining the biomass composition and relating it with carbon dot (CDs) formation. For this reason, the great weakness in studies involving the green synthesis of CDs is the measurement of the constituents of the raw biomass, the final product, and the solid by-product, limited only to operational parameters such as reaction time and temperature. It is, therefore, essential to evaluate the contribution of the solids load and the reaction temperature in the final properties of the product formed [3,24,29].

The quality and properties of the CDs depend on the composition of the biomass and on ensuring the carbonization and conversion of these constituents into CDs. Therefore, based on studies in the literature, it is challenging to understand how CDs are formed. For this reason, it is necessary to quantify the constituents present at each stage of CDs formation in terms of mass balance. Data on the quantification of the initial biomass, the percentage of conversion into CDs, and the final yield in mass terms of the freeze-dried nanoparticle should be addressed in these studies.

Another gap in previous studies related to CDs from RH is the investigation of the kinetics of CD formation since high reaction times can often be unnecessary, leading to high energy costs and increasing the cost of the process. For this reason, the kinetics investigation is fundamental for optimizing chemical reactions, allowing the ideal reaction time to be identified for the efficient formation of CDs. Evaluating kinetics also allows understanding the speed and mechanisms involved in converting precursors into CDs, making it an essential factor in maximizing the production and quality of nanomaterials.

For example, Ogi et al. [30] carried out a kinetic study of the formation of nitrogen-doped CDs using citric acid and urea as precursors. Another study used citric acid and Citrus japonica (Japanese lemon) extract to investigate the kinetics of CD formation using a microwave route, focusing on the synthesis parameters (power and heating time) and their influence on the optical properties without detailing the formation of the material from the composition of the precursor used [31]. Balakrishnan et al. [32] followed the same prescribed approach, evaluating the mechanism of CD formation using palm kernel shells as a precursor via microwaves, highlighting the influence of the microwave irradiation time (30 s to 120 s) and the concentration of the doping agent on the fluorescence properties of the CDs. Other studies that look at kinetics from the perspective of the materials have been reported [33–35].

Herein, the engineering and mechanistic insights of carbon dot formation from rice husks were uncovered using the hydrothermal route. Characterization of the raw RH, the solid by-product of pre-treatment, and the R-CDs formed were considered. Firstly, a statistical analysis was conducted to determine the best condition for obtaining CDs, using the removal of organic compounds from the solid fraction and the DC as the response variable. Later, the optimum experimental design condition was used to study the kinetics of R-CDs formation to thoroughly investigate the reaction time for converting organic compounds into carbon dots. Finally, the viability of the R-CDs synthesized from rice husk was analyzed in relation to the detection of atrazine, a widely used toxic pesticide. This work showed that the optimal kinetic condition was achieved with 4 h of reaction, 220 °C and 3 % w/v of solid load. Furthermore, a mechanism proposal was presented focusing on the composition of rice husk, where experimental evidence of hemicellulose and cellulose pathways was observed by identifying some monomers, which suggests that this can open ring, condensation, and finally, carbonization/aromatization to form carbon dots. Other pathways, for example, from lignin and extractives, were suggested without strong experimental evidence. However, reducing these components suggests their participation in carbon dot formation. Thus, investigating the mechanism and kinetics of R-CDs formation using green synthesis is still a major challenge. Therefore, to cover the question that this work doesn’t answer, additional experiments with macromolecules/monomers from rice husk will be addressed in the future. However, we hope that this in-depth study of the formation of R-CDs using biomass of global importance, rice husks, will encourage further research in the area. Adding value and promoting the circular economy of waste that is disposed of incorrectly is the great challenge facing science today.

The rice husk used in this study was obtained from the company Rofertil (Santo Antônio de Posse, São Paulo, Brazil), which was ground using a Willey knife mill (DeLeo, Porto Alegre, Brazil) to a particle size of 30 mesh. Distilled water was used to synthesize and prepare the solutions.

The salt buffer solutions (pH 2; pH 3 citric acid/disodium phosphate; pH 4 potassium biftalate/sodium hydroxide; pH 5 citric acid/disodium phosphate; pH 6; pH 7 disodium phosphate/monobasic potassium phosphate; pH 8; pH 9 boric acid/potassium chloride; pH 10 boric acid/potassium chloride and pH 11 disodium phosphate/sodium hydroxide) were purchased from Neon. The agrochemicals glyphosate, mesotrione, amicarbazone, atrazine, imidacloprid, dicamba, diuron, and acephate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich grade Pestanal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design for the optimized removal of organic compounds and degradation products from rice husk
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal synthesis from RH was conducted using a high-pressure stainless-steel reactor (Model RTA-BB-450-CM, METALQUIM, Brazil) with a 450 mL capacity, maximum operating temperature of 300 °C, a heating power of 1.0 kW, and a maximum working pressure of 138 bar (2.000 psi). To investigate the release of compounds originating from the lignocellulosic structure of the rice husk for the reactional medium, we employed a 22 Central Composite Rotational Design (CCRD). This design consisted of 4 tests under axial conditions and three repetitions at the central point, resulting in 11 experimental runs. The independent variables considered were temperature (149.64, 160, 185, 210, and 220.36 °C), denoted as X1, and solid load (2.93, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 17.07 m/v), represented as X2. The dependent variables were: (1) the percentage of organic compound removal (ROC) from RH (%, w[cellulose + hemicellulose + lignin + protein + extractives]/w[total organic compounds]) to the liquid fraction during the hydrothermal reaction; and (2) the percentage of DC (%, w[xylose + glycose + arabinose + cellobiose + acetic acid + formic acid + FF + HMF]/w[polysaccharides]) formed during the reaction [36].

The hydrothermal synthesis was done by feeding the reactor with biomass and distilled water in predetermined proportions, as specified in the experimental design. The reactions were conducted at 500 rpm and maintained for 4 h, counted from when the proposed temperature was reached under each condition [24]. After the completion of the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature, and the dark brown mixture was centrifuged in a Centribio 80-2B centrifuge for 30 min at 5.000 g. Afterward, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm cellulose acetate membrane to quantification of DC and TS such as cellobiose, glucose, xylose, arabinose, furfural, HMF, acetic acid, and formic acid by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The retained material (solid residue) was subsequently dried in an oven at 105 °C for 24 h to constant weight, and its lignocellulosic composition was characterized by cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, and ash content.

The results were statistically analyzed (as detailed in section 2.3.3) to identify the optimal conditions that maximize the removal of organic compounds and the production of RH degradation products, which are crucial factors in CD formation [37]. Following the determination of temperature and solid load parameters, a kinetic analysis was conducted to assess the effect of time on the formation of TS, DC, and QY, as described in section 2.3.

A kinetic study of R-CD formation was conducted following the modified methodology [36]. An analysis was performed by varying the time (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 h) under isothermal conditions, with the solid load and temperature (both optimized) kept constant, as well as the agitation (500 rpm). Time zero in the kinetic analysis corresponds to the moment when the isothermal temperature is reached. The experiments were performed in duplicate, and after the synthesis was completed, the reaction medium was treated as specified in section 2.2. The liquid fraction was analyzed, assessing the profile of DC formation (%, w[acetic acid + formic acid + FF + HMF]/w[polysaccharides]) and TS (%, w[xylose + glucose + arabinose + cellobiose]/w[polysaccharides]).

The QY was also evaluated at each kinetic point to understand the mechanisms of R-CD formation during the hydrothermal process. The results were statistically analyzed to select the time that provided the highest QY. The yield profile (% w/w) and concentration (g/L) of TS and DC were analyzed for each molecule individually, to establish correlations with the QY profile. This allowed for identifying the relationship between CD formation and the evolution of DC and TS over time. At the end of the kinetics study, an evaluation was conducted to identify the optimal conditions, considering the time, temperature, and optimized solid load to maximize R-CD production. The material obtained from this synthesis was characterized according to the procedures described in section 2.5.

The calculation of the severity factor was used to assess the intensity of the hydrothermal synthesis process of R-CDs and to understand the impact of the time–temperature combination on the structural changes of the raw RH in each run (Eq. 1). This mathematical model describes the behavior of the three phases of the hydrothermal process (Fig. S1), which include heating, isothermal period, and cooling [38]. The severity factor allows the determination of process conditions by combining time and temperature into a single variable, facilitating scale-up stages and comparison between different conditions. The correlation between the severity factor and the yields of DC, QY, and TS was analyzed to identify the log(Ro) corresponding to the most effective condition observed in this study, as reported by [36].

The chemical composition of the raw RH and the solid streams resulting from the hydrothermal synthesis in all runs were characterized. The content of extractives was determined through sequential extraction with ethanol and water, following the procedure outlined in the National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL/TP-510–42619) [40]. Cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, and ash were quantified using the method provided in NREL/TP-510–42618 [41]. Protein content was quantified using the Kjeldahl method, following the standard methodology established by [42].

The contents of solids (S), glucan (G), xylan (X), lignin (L), and organic compounds (OC) removed in the solid fraction after hydrothermal synthesis were calculated following Equations 2, 3, and 4, as described in [38,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: QY determination and quantification of sugars and DC
ce:section.ce:para: For the analysis of the relative QY in the kinetics stage, the R-CDs were dialyzed using a cellulose acetate membrane (cut-off 1000 Da, 1 kDa) in ultrapure water for 72 h [44]. Measurements were performed in triplicate.

The liquid fraction obtained after the hydrothermal synthesis of RH was subjected to the analysis of fluorescence QY of R-CDs using a quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Q = 0.54 %) as a reference due to the spectrum's similarity with the synthesized material [45], at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm [24], following Equation 7.
(7)

where Q is the QY, n is the refractive index, A is the absorbance, and I is the measured integrated emission intensity. In addition, the indices “st” and “x” show the relative value of the standard material and the CDs, respectively. Quinine sulphate (Φ R = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33), while R-CDs were dissolved in distilled water (η = 1.33) in preparation for the measurements. The absorbance in a 1 cm quartz cuvette was kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm to minimize the resorption effect [46].

Another portion of the liquid fraction underwent ultra-fast liquid chromatography (UFLC) analysis to determine glucose, xylose, arabinose, cellobiose, acetic acid, formic acid, and furfural. The methodology was based on the work of [38], utilizing a Shimadzu Prominence LC-20A chromatograph (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a Bio-rad® Aminex HPX-87H column (300 × 7.8 mm) and a refractive index detector RID-20A. A 10 μL sample injection was eluted with 5 mmol/L H2SO4 at a 0.6 mL/min flow rate. The oven temperature was maintained at 65 °C, and the total run time was 50 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis of hydrothermal synthesis of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
A Response Surface Methodology (RSM) coupled with multiple regression was applied to model the synthesis of R-CDs in terms of QY. The statistical significance of the effects was verified through ANOVA, and non-significant effects were excluded from the initial model. Experimental data were re-adjusted only for significant parameters (p ≤ 0.05). The adequacy and quality of the fit were assessed using the Lack of Fit test, the coefficient of determination (R2), and its adjusted R2. The generalized model utilized in the RSM is presented in Equation 6. In this equation, Y represents the predicted response, while β0, βi, βii, and βij correspond to the regression coefficients for the intercept, linear, quadratic, and interaction terms, respectively. Xi and Xj denote the independent variables. Once the mathematical model was obtained, multi-response optimization using the desirability function [47] was employed to maximize the highest QY condition. The execution of the CCRD and data analysis were conducted using Statistica v.13.5 (T.I.B.C.O. Software Inc., Palo Alto, CA, USA).

The R-CDs obtained in the optimal reaction kinetics condition were subjected to dialysis with a cellulose acetate membrane (1000 Da, 1 kDa cut-off) in ultrapure water for 72 h [44] to purify them. After this process, the R-CDs were characterized.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify functional groups in an Infrared Spectrophotometer Agilent Technologies − Cary 660 FTIR model, between 400 cm−1 and 4000 cm−1. The sample was prepared in KBr tablets with 2 % (w/w). Subsequently, the sample was pressed to obtain a circular tablet. S.A.Origin Pro 8.5 software (OriginLab, Northampton, Massachusetts, USA) was used to analyze characteristic bands of the FTIR spectra.

TEM micrographs were obtained on a JEM-1011 electron microscope with 100 kV resolution. Sample preparation consisted of diluting a small amount of the material in 10 mL of isopropyl alcohol for deagglomeration in an ultrasonic bath. The supernatant was dripped using a precision pipette (100 μL) onto copper grids covered with amorphous carbon film (GRIDS) suitable for TEM analysis. ImageJ® software was used to process the images and estimate particle size.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed at Rigaku MiniFlex600 XRD equipment under Cu − Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) and 2θ angle ranging from 10˚ to 60˚. Emission, excitation, and absorption spectra were collected using the Infinite M200 multi-reader UV–Vis dual beam spectrophotometer (Tecan) to examine the optical properties of the synthesized R-CDs. In the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) analysis, absorption readings were between 200 and 800 nm wavelengths. The photostability tests of the R-CDs followed the protocol described by Guo et al. [48], with modifications. Briefly, the experiments were carried out at room temperature, using UV radiation with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, in a TECAN spectrophotometer for 24 h. The measurements were taken in triplicate at 60-min intervals.

A zeta potential analysis was conducted to investigate the particles' surface charge and colloidal stability (Malvern® Zetasizer Nano, ZS90). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to identify elemental composition and chemical states on the surface of materials using a Thermo Scientific K-alpha instrument with an Al Kα monochromatic X-ray source and an energy step size of 0.025 eV. A C1s level of 284.8 eV was used as an internal standard. CasaXPS software was used to calculate the elemental composition.

100 µL of R-CDs (pH 10) and 100 µL of atrazine in different concentrations were used for the detection tests. After incubation for 12 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with excitation at 360 nm [49–51]. Selectivity in the detection of atrazine was assessed by adding other pesticides such as glyphosate, mesotrione, amicarbazone, imidacloprid, dicamba, diuron, and acephate, each at a concentration of 500 µmol/L, as possible interferents, in the mixture with the R-CDs probe [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of hydrothermal synthesis on rice husk composition
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical composition of the raw RH was as follows: 32.89 ± 0.52 % cellulose, 12.82 ± 0.44 % hemicellulose (comprising 12.4 % arabinoxylan and 0.4 % acetyl groups), 30.03 ± 0.54 % lignin (containing 22.3 % insoluble lignin and 7.8 % soluble lignin), 5.10 ± 0.64 % extractives, 20.48 ± 0.50 % total ash (comprising 2.9 % acid-soluble ash and 17.6 % acid-insoluble ash), and 3.48 ± 0.15 % protein. The results obtained in this study corroborate with previous works [34,35], which report ranges of 34–36 % cellulose, 11–14 % hemicellulose, 20–36 % lignin, and 16–22 % ash.

In Table 1 , the effects of hydrothermal synthesis on the chemical composition of the solid fraction under each experimental condition are demonstrated. Temperature notably influenced the structure of the biomass polysaccharides. When the temperature reached around 220.36 °C, only 19.01 % of cellulose remained in the solid fraction, with no hemicellulose and a high concentration of residual lignin and ash. This indicated that 64 % of the glucan and 100 % of the xylan were solubilized into the liquid fraction. A similar trend is observed for temperatures exceeding 210 °C, resulting in higher polysaccharide removal yields in the solid fraction.

On the other hand, greater preservation of the polysaccharide structure of rice husk (RH) was observed at lower temperatures. For example, in run 5 (149.64 °C and 10 % w/v), almost complete preservation of glucan and low depolymerization of xylan was observed, resulting in percentages of 39.23 % and 11.01 % for cellulose and hemicellulose, respectively. At temperatures between 160 °C and 185 °C, cellulose ranged from 30 % to 45 %, while hemicellulose ranged from 2 % to 11 %. As expected, hydrothermal reactions were more effective in solubilizing the hemicellulose fraction, leading to lower percentages of this fraction in the structural composition and higher removal yields. These results suggest that the relative proportions of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin may influence the formation of R-CDs, as hydrothermal synthesis is more effective in hydrolyzing specific polymers, such as hemicellulose, and more challenging in degrading cellulose and lignin. The combination of high temperatures with low solid load appeared to have a more pronounced effect on hemicellulose, while positively impacting cellulose. This behavior suggests that controlling these variables may be an effective strategy for optimizing R-CDs formation.

The role of high temperature and pressure in hydrothermal reactions further supports these observations, as water ionization generates hydronium ions that initiate the depolymerization of polysaccharides [52,53]. This process selectively hydrolyzes ether linkages and acetyl groups, particularly in hemicellulose, but may also affect cellulose depending on the reaction severity [52,53]. Organic acids released during these reactions catalyze the hydrolysis process, transforming polysaccharide fractions into simpler molecules like celooligosaccharides and arabino-xilooligosaccharides, which can be further converted into glucose, xylose, and arabinose [38,52,53]. These molecules can eventually form hydroxymethylfurfural and furfural compounds that further condense into aromatic structures, constituting R-CDs backbone [54]. This process reinforces the importance of controlling reaction parameters, such as temperature and solid load, to optimize the conversion of polysaccharides into R-CDs.

With its high carbon content, lignin is crucial in producing CDs [55]. The hydrothermal syntheses demonstrated that a portion of the lignin solubilized into the liquid phase, with solubilization rates ranging from 0 % to 58 %. The highest lignin removal yield (57.75 %) was achieved in Run 7, which used the lowest solid load, while the lowest removal was observed at temperatures above 200 °C. Interestingly, higher removal yields were recorded at lower temperatures (149.64–185 °C), likely due to the degradation of unstable lignin components into smaller, soluble molecules, facilitating quantification under milder conditions. In the case of rice husk (RH), unstable components represent 7.8 % of the lignin (acid-soluble), which may be solubilized during hydrothermal synthesis. In contrast, at higher temperatures and pressures, the more stable regions of lignin (22.3 % insoluble lignin), along with other organic molecules present in the biomass (such as proteins, extracts, cellulose, and hemicellulose), are converted into a thermally stable solid monolith [36,55]. These monoliths are subsequently identified as “lignin” in the chemical characterization of the streams, which may influence the quantification of lignin removal, as described by [36].

Organic compounds present in the raw RH include the protein fraction (3.48 %) and the extractives fraction (5.10 %), which could have been converted into R-CDs since the presence of these compounds was not detected in the analyzed runs. In hydrothermal synthesis, proteins can undergo amino acid decarboxylation, producing carbonic acids and amines. Furthermore, amino acid deamination generates ammonia and organic acids, which can be precursors in generating R-CDs [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of hydrothermal synthesis on the solubilization of compounds
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the chemical composition of the liquid fraction, including TS, DC, and the severity factor after hydrothermal synthesis, are presented in Table 2 . Quantified DC exhibited higher levels compared to sugars in most runs. As previously mentioned, the hemicellulose polysaccharide chain is more sensitive to hydrothermal synthesis and, therefore, more easily hydrolyzed. Due to this effect, the concentration of furfural (a byproduct of xylose degradation) showed higher levels than xylose in runs conducted at temperatures above 185 °C.

This phenomenon may be related to the severity level to which RH has been subjected and its positive impact on the formation of quantum compounds [38,54]. Under harsher conditions, xylan was degraded entirely (See Table 1), and over time, xylose underwent dehydration to form anhydrous xylose, which was subsequently oxidized to generate furfural [38]. In parallel, condensation, chain fragmentation, and chain-forming reactions may occur to produce cyclic compounds [54]. These compounds then undergo polymerization and condensation, resulting in aromatics with oxygen-containing functional groups, which can be precursors to R-CDs [54].

Low concentrations of glucose and cellobiose, derived from the cellulose structure of RH, were observed in the liquid fraction after hydrothermal synthesis. Cellulose experienced the least depolymerization among the polysaccharides, but, as indicated in Table 1, significant degradation of this polysaccharide occurred under more severe conditions. The phenomenon observed in the hemicellulose fraction may extend to the cellulose fraction, where low sugar concentrations can positively influence the production of R-CDs. Similar to what was observed with xylose, glucose units may have been converted into HMF, which was then further dehydrated into aromatic carbonaceous structures, contributing to the carbon backbone of R-CDs [54]. Glucose is isomerized into fructose, followed by eliminating three water molecules to form HMF, which is then dehydrated and polymerized into small polymeric nano agglomerates. Over time, the formation of carbonaceous spheres takes place [56].

During the synthesis of R-CDs from lignocellulosic biomass, the concentration of DC generated by the thermal decomposition of macromolecules (cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin) is critical. Under high-temperature conditions, the biomass is decomposed into smaller intermediates, such as sugars and DC, which serve as precursors for CD formation. The efficiency of CD nucleation and growth is directly related to the concentration of these intermediates, impacting the conversion of biomass into R-CDs. Therefore, understanding the phenomena involved in hydrothermal synthesis, such as organic compound removal (ROC) and degradation compound formation (DC), is essential for optimizing reaction parameters and maximizing the transformation of biomass into R-CDs. Table 3 presents the effects of temperature and solid load on removing organic compounds (ROC) and forming DC during hydrothermal synthesis. It is observed that, for all analyzed models, the ROC and DC variables were highly significant (p < 0.001) and did not exhibit a lack of fit (p > 0.05). Furthermore, the model explained over 95 % of the data variability, with an adjusted R-squared greater than 93 %, indicating a good fit to the experimental data.

Regarding the model effects on ROC, temperature showed positive linear (X1) effects and negative quadratic effects (X1 2). Conversely, solid load significantly negatively affected ROC, both linear (X2) and quadratic (X2 2). Overall, this suggests that higher temperatures can significantly enhance the removal of organic compounds from rice husks. In contrast, the model indicates that higher solid loads tend to decrease the efficiency of organic compound removal from rice husk. For the formation of DC in the reaction medium during hydrothermal synthesis, the model showed that the linear regression coefficient for temperature (X1) had significantly positive effects. In contrast, solid load exhibited significantly negative linear and quadratic effects on DC formation, similar to its impact on ROC. The effects of temperature and solid load on ROC and DC are illustrated on the response surface presented in Fig. 1 .

As demonstrated in Table 3, high solid loads can adversely affect ROC and DC yields due to heat and mass transfer limitations, leading to medium saturation during hydrothermal synthesis [38]. However, these limitations can be mitigated by increasing the temperature, which significantly enhances the decomposition of RH. Statistical analysis reveals that optimal conditions are achieved with low solid loads (3 % w/v) and high temperatures (220 °C), maximizing ROC and DC yields in the reaction medium (Fig. 1). These results corroborate the findings of Wongso et al. [57], who indicated that higher temperatures, up to 225 °C, are ideal for efficient carbonization, resulting in carbon dots with enhanced fluorescent properties [57]. Thus, optimal conditions of high temperature and lower biomass load promote more uniform degradation, facilitating mass transfer and the formation and growth of R-CDs.

Following optimization, the ideal condition (220 °C – 3 % w/v) was subjected to a kinetic study to evaluate the release profile of DC, total solids, and QY over time. During this phase, the profile of sugars and DC was monitored regarding yield and concentration. Additionally, an analysis based on the severity factor was conducted to assess the impact of the experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of hydrothermal synthesis for the formation of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration and yield of total solids solubilized in the reaction medium during the kinetic study varied from 0.51 g/L and 3.72 % w/w at 0 h (Fig. 2 -A). As the exposure time at 220 °C increased, there was a gradual decrease in TS levels, reaching 0.11 g/L and 0.80 % w/w by the end of the 12-h reaction period. Additionally, among all the sugars, xylose, derived from hemicellulose, was undetectable after 4 h. Similarly, cellobiose, a sugar derived from cellulose, was no longer detectable after 8 h. This trend can be attributed to the preferential degradation of hemicellulose during hydrothermal synthesis, which solubilizes this polysaccharide into its monomers early in the reaction. Over time, xylose was further degraded, subsequently appearing as furfural. A similar trend of decreasing TS with time was observed by [36] in the hydrothermal synthesis of tobacco stems.

The R-CDs showed no significant differences in average concentrations throughout the kinetic study, as shown in Table S1 and Fig. 2-B. This indicates that the R-CD levels remained stable over the entire kinetic period. Acetic acid was the predominant compound, starting at 0.83 g/L and 6.02 % w/w at 0 h, and increasing to 1.64 g/L and 11.96 % w/w after 12 h. Acetic acid, like xylose, is derived from hemicellulose, which may explain its high concentration at all time points in the kinetics.

The kinetics of R-CDs formation were investigated to assess how reaction time (ranging from 0 to 12 h) affects the QY. The analysis revealed a parabolic decreasing trend in the relationship between reaction time and QY, as shown in Fig. 2c. Initially, the QY increases gradually, reaching a maximum value of 19.28 ± 0.28 after 8 h of reaction (Table S1). However, a significant reduction is observed beyond this point, with the QY decreasing to 4.95 ± 1.31 after 12 h of reaction. A series of interrelated factors can explain this pattern.

At the beginning of the reaction, the high temperature of 220 °C promotes the thermal degradation of the organic constituents present in rice husk, such as cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, breaking these macromolecules into smaller molecules and forming a carbonized material. These carbonized molecules aggregate, giving rise to the initial R-CDs. During this initial formation, functional groups such as hydroxyls (–OH) and carboxyls (–COOH) can attach to the CD structure, passivating its surface and enhancing its optical properties, contributing to the increase in QY and stability [58,59]. The observed behavior is consistent with the findings of Li et al. [60], where the biomass composition significantly influenced the fluorescence properties of the carbon dots following hydrothermal treatment.

The decline in QY after 8 h of reaction can be attributed to the prolonged reaction time, which leads to the degradation of the formed R-CDs. Continuous exposure to high temperatures can cause bond rupture, resulting in the fragmentation of the R-CDs and the loss of their luminescent properties [61]. Thermal degradation of R-CDs can lead to the formation of structural defects that act as trapping centers for excited electrons. As the size of the R-CDs increases, these defects become more prevalent, facilitating the trapping of non-radiative electrons [62]. This process reduces the probability of photon emission, thereby decreasing both the particles' QY and the excited-state lifetime, as observed by Barman and Patra [63].

Prolonged reaction times can also promote the agglomeration of R-CDs, leading to the formation of larger aggregates. These larger aggregates have a reduced surface area, which diminishes light interaction and, consequently, the QY of the R-CDs [64]. QY for reaction times of 4, 6, 8, and 10 h did not show significant differences (p ≤ 0.05), suggesting that 4 h is the optimal synthesis condition (220 °C − 3 % w/v), considering cost, practicality, and scalability. These findings are consistent with the literature, which also reports the effective synthesis of carbon dots within this time [65,66].

Therefore, as highlighted by [53], the severity factor is a crucial parameter for evaluating the intensity of a hydrothermal synthesis process, providing insights into how temperature and time conditions affect reactivity and efficiency. This parameter enables the determination of optimal conditions from a single variable, facilitating process scaling. As illustrated in Fig. 2-D, the concentration of TS decreases as the severity factor increases, while the levels of DC and QY increase correspondingly (Table S2 and S3). When the severity factor reaches 6.22, indicating the optimal process conditions, the highest QY is observed, associated with a low concentration of TS and a plateau in DC levels.

Therefore, the optimum kinetic condition was reached with 4 h of reaction, 220 °C, and 3 % w/v solid charge. The R-CDs obtained from this condition were dialyzed (1 kDa cut-off membrane, Fig. S2) and subjected to characterization, the details of which will be presented in the following section.

The R-CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal synthesis, in which the organic precursor is subjected to high pressure and temperature conditions, promoting dehydration, fragmentation, condensation, aromatization, and carbonization to form the nanoparticles [67]. When rice husk biomass is mixed with water, the initial mixture pH is 6.75. After being subjected to the hydrothermal process, numerous reactions take place under autogenous pressure, in which the reaction pH is altered to the acidic range, with pH varying in the range 3.32 to 3.49 (Table S1 of the supplementary material).

This process transforms the organic biomass into R-CDs, activating functional groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl, which give the R-CDs excellent hydrophilic and photoluminescent properties, increasing their versatility for various applications [68]. The synthesis yield values in terms of R-CDs mass per rice husk can be graphically represented. As shown in Fig. 3 and Table S4 (Supplementary Material), the mass yield of the R-CDs increases initially since these conditions favor the efficient formation of the nanoparticles. However, after a certain reaction time, there is a decline in the mass yield, possibly due to the degradation of the R-CDs as the reaction continues [61].

Fig. 4 a shows the infrared spectra of the R-CDs. The FTIR spectrum recorded characteristic chemical bonds in carbon quantum dots synthesized from rice husk [69]. The intense and broad band around 3201 cm−1 is characteristic of the O-H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups, justifying the R-CDs' highly hydrophilic character [20].

The band around 1567 cm−1 is characteristic of the stretching vibrations of non-conjugated linear alkenes (C=C) [70]. The band in this spectrum region confirms the presence of graphitic carbon in the sp2 domains of the carbon in the structure of R-CDs [70]. The oxygen-containing functional groups were also identified in the region around 1076 cm−1 spectra, related to the C-O-C stretching vibration in alcohols, 1636 cm−1 related to de C=O bonds, and 1404 cm−1 related to the O=C-O bonds [69].

The spectra recorded between 1000–400 cm−1 reveal evidence of the residual presence of the Si element from the rice husk since the thermal decomposition of the rice husk may not eliminate the silicon present in the husk, causing part of this silicon to be incorporated into the structure of the R-CDs [71]. For this reason, the bands in 790 cm−1 and 457 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the Si − O − Si bonds and the band in 953 cm−1 refers to the Si-OH bond [72].

As seen in the biomass characterization, the main constituents of rice husks are cellulose (28.59 %), lignin (30.03 %), and ash (20.48 %). The main ash component is silicon dioxide (SiO2), which the band corroborates in this region [73].

Fig. 4b shows the diffractogram of the synthesized R-CDs, in which it is possible to confirm the amorphous nature through the broad peak at 2θ = 23.4° attributed to reflection from the (0 0 2) plane and an interlayer spacing of 0.379 nm. As the interlayer distance of the carbon dots obtained (0.379 nm) is greater than that of graphite (0.335 nm), it can be inferred that the R-CDs do not have a typical graphitic structure and are not very crystalline, as observed in the diffractogram. This difference in interplanar distance suggests that R-CDs have a different structural organization, with a lower degree of crystalline order than graphite. This behavior may be related to the constituents of the rice husk used in the synthesis [74], and the formation of polymers derived from carbohydrates and lignin during the hydrothermal reaction, which are amorphous [75]. Pandey et al. [27], Mahmoud et al. [23] and Jothi et al.[24] obtained similar values for the interlayer distance of R-CDs synthesized from rice husk biomass, with values of 0.377 nm, 0.37 nm, and 0.40 nm, respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was used to determine the chemical structure and electronic state of the elements in the CDs synthesized from rice husk biomass, illustrated in Fig. 5 . As can be seen from the full scan XPS spectra (Fig. 5a), the R-CDs showed 4 distinct peaks at binding energies of 103 eV, 150 eV, 285 eV, and 533 eV, which correspond to Si 2p, Si 2 s, C 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively [76]. These peaks indicate the presence of silicon, carbon, and oxygen elements in the R-CDs, with atomic ratios of 23.39 %, 25.92 %, and 49.96 %, respectively. Nitrogen had no visible peak in the spectrum due to its low atomic percentage, 0.73 %.

Fig. 5b shows the high-resolution deconvolution spectrum of C1s in the range 280–292 eV. Different peaks appeared at 284.52, 284.81, 286.05, and 287.37 eV, corresponding to C=C, C-C, C-O-C, and C=O bonds [77]. Similarly, Fig. 5c shows that the deconvoluted spectrum of O1s provides peaks at 531.38, 532.18, 533.35, 533.73, and 534.03 eV corresponding to C=O, C-O, O=C-O, C-O-C and C-OH groups, respectively [78,79].

The nitrogen content in the R-CDs was not detected in relevant quantities, which was attributed to the rice husk components. According to the literature, rice husks contain around 0.05–4.26 % nitrogen in their overall elemental composition [80]. Using rice husks as biomass for synthesizing R-CDs leads to nitrogen in low concentrations in the material. This result is consistent with the characterization of the biomass composition with a low protein content in the rice husk (3.48 %). The results are consistent with Abidin et al. [26], who synthesized carbon dots via a hydrothermal process from rice husks, obtaining CDs with a nitrogen content of 1.52–1.92 % without functionalization.

The high-resolution spectrum for Si 2p (Fig. 5d) showed peaks at 102.98 eV, 103.58 eV, and 103.95 eV that correspond to Si-O-C, Si-O-Si, and Si-OH bonds, respectively [72]. Therefore, the XPS results corroborate the bands identified in the FTIR spectrum, with a typical carbonaceous structure and surface groups containing oxygen and silicon from the biomass composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of optical properties of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties were analyzed using the synthesized R-CDs UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra, as shown in Fig. 6 . The formation of the nanoparticles was confirmed by the change in color from brown in visible light to blue after irradiation with 365 nm UV light (Fig. 6). This behavior was observed in other recent works [22,25,81] using RH as biomass to produce R-CDs.

The absorbance spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 6a) showed a peak at 275 nm corresponding to the π-π* transitions of the sp2 domains of the carbon [22]. Fig. 6b shows the emission spectra of the R-CDs, where the nanoparticles were excited in a range from 320 nm to 500 nm. The light intensity of the curves gradually increased as the excitation length was changed, a behavior strongly related to the presence of functional groups on the surface of the R-CDs [82]. This contributes to the fluorescence emission of the synthesized R-CDs due to radiative recombination between holes and electrons mediated by photoinduced charge separation and trapping at surface sites [83].
The excitation wavelength at 360 nm showed the highest fluorescence, with maximum emission centered on 445 nm, corresponding to the blue emission wavelength (Fig. 6c).

The R-CDs showed good photostability (Fig. S3), and the results are consistent with studies in the literature using rice husk as a precursor. Excitation wavelengths varied in the 320–360 nm range, and maximum emission was in the 430–466 nm range [3,22,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological properties and zeta potential of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows TEM micrographs of the synthesized R-CDs, which are approximately spherical particles with good particle dispersion and an average size of 2.17 ± 0.58 nm (Fig. 7d). The values obtained are consistent with the main characteristic of R-CDs, i.e., a size of less than 10 nm [84].

In addition, zeta potential analysis was carried out to determine the electrical charges on the surface of the nanoparticles and their colloidal stability (Fig. 7e). The zeta potential value for the synthesized R-CDs was −25.7 mV, which indicates the presence of negatively charged ionizable functional groups such as carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) on their surface [85]. The FTIR spectrum in the 1400 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1 regions also corroborated this.

This zeta potential value suggests that the R-CDs have significant electrostatic repulsion between them, preventing aggregation [86], which results in a homogeneous and stable dispersion in the suspension, as seen in the TEM images (Fig. 7 a-c).

Proposing a mechanism for the formation of carbon dots from biomass that has several compounds with a high probability of generating R-CDs rich in functional groups is a major challenge. The process of forming R-CDs is intrinsically complex, involving multiple factors. Even so, the data presented shows a consistent convergence and corroborates each other, reinforcing the validity of the observations. For this reason, an experimental design was first carried out to investigate the influence of the synthesis parameters, such as temperature and solids load, on the carbonization of the biomass, characterized by the release of the degradation compounds (Fig. 8 ).

From the degradation profile of the biomass components, it can be inferred that polysaccharides, extractives, and proteins are the main carbon sources and that their degradation results in the formation of R-CDs. The increase in temperature favored the decomposition of polysaccharides into lower molecular weight molecules through various condensation, depolymerization, and carbonization reactions that resulted in the formation of R-CDs. As the temperature increases, the sugars such as glucose, arabinose, cellobiose, xylose, and cellobiose degrade further into lower molecular weight molecules and form R-CDs through various carbonization reactions inside the reactor.

According to Table 2, tests 6 and 7, which used the highest temperature (220 °C) and the lowest solids load (3 % w/v), generated the highest quantities of degradation compounds. These results show the influence of the reaction parameters on the formation of degradation products, which are directly related to the degree of carbonization of the biomass. The increase in the release of these compounds reflects more efficient carbonization, promoting more effective conversion of the organic precursor into R-CDs, the product of interest.

The reaction temperature is strongly associated with the carbonization process as described in the literature, since high temperatures intensify this process. According to Zhang et al. [87], as the degree of carbonization increases, there is a tendency to form structures more similar to graphene, constituting R-CDs structure. During hydrothermal synthesis, the increase in temperature facilitates the thermal degradation of the lignocellulosic components of biomass (cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin), forming carbon-rich molecular precursors.

The data in Table 1 is consistent with the visual appearance of the R-CDs after the freeze-drying process (Fig. S4). The highest synthesis temperature (sixth condition of the experimental design) revealed a pronounced dark brown coloration as a visual indication of a higher degree of carbonization. In contrast, the less intense coloring (light brown) of the R-CDs obtained at the lowest temperature (149,64 °C, condition 5 of the design) reflects a lower degree of carbonization.

However, very high temperatures can cause defects in the properties of carbon dots and thereby affect their optical properties since an excessive increase in temperature results in a decrease in fluorescence intensity due to the rise in non-radiative decay processes. Studies show that very high temperatures induce the formation of structural defects, which act as capture centers for excited electrons, favoring non-radiative emission and, consequently, reducing the quantum yield and optical properties of carbon dots [88]. Wongso et al. [57] verified this effect by observing that excessive carbonization of rice husk occurs at temperatures above 225 °C, reducing the performance of the optical properties of carbon dots. These findings align with other studies using the same biomass in the literature [24,25,89].

In addition, the synthesis condition (Fig. 1) that revealed the highest biomass conversion was at the lowest solid loading (3 % w/v) with the highest amount of degradation products (Table 2), whose value was 21.13 % w/w. This condition balances the availability of precursors for the formation of R-CDs while avoiding saturation of the reaction medium, promoting a homogeneous dispersion of the lignocellulosic components in the system. This improves heat and mass transfer, fundamental factors for thermal degradation reactions, aromatization, and carbonized structures' nucleation. In addition, the smaller mass in the reactor reduces the formation of large agglomerates, which could favor a higher surface-to-volume ratio. Thus, reaction interactions become more efficient, minimizing temperature gradients and the risk of aggregation and incomplete carbonization, promoting greater structural uniformity and superior optical properties in the R-CDs.

The kinetic study made it possible to determine the ideal synthesis time under the optimized conditions, as shown in Fig. 8. Based on the results obtained and the literature that covers in-depth studies of the components analyzed, we propose a mechanism illustrated in Fig. 9 that describes the potential route for converting cellulose and hemicellulose into carbon dots.

Initially, we proposed that the cellulose and hemicellulose macromolecules go through a depolymerization stage, where the polymer chains break down, generating smaller sugars [90]. The production of these sugars was proven by HPLC analysis, as shown in Table 2. Next, we suggest that these smaller sugars undergo a ring-opening process, forming even smaller molecules. Although not wholly identified in this study, these molecules may undergo condensation reactions, culminating in a complex macromolecular structure. This final stage involves carbonization and aromatization processes, resulting in the production of R-CDs.

In addition, we considered lignin, an abundant component in the rice husk used as a precursor, which was converted during the reaction. According to the literature, lignin can be degraded under the experimental conditions used in this study, giving rise to monomers that potentially participate in the subsequent steps in forming R-CDs [90]. These mechanistic insights offer an initial perspective on the possible reaction pathways involved in synthesizing carbon dots from lignocellulosic biomass, contributing to understanding the underlying processes.

The monomers suggested in this mechanism were not detected at any stage of this work. The analyses did not identify the presence of these compounds, indicating that they may be consumed directly in the condensation process, contributing to the formation of the proposed macrostructure. This macrostructure, in turn, undergoes subsequent stages of carbonization and aromatization, producing carbon dots.

Rice husk, used as a precursor, is rich in minor compounds, including phenols, proteins, extractives, and hydrated silica, which is typical of this material. These compounds, especially phenolics, have similar chemical characteristics to lignin monomers and can also take part in condensation reactions, forming intermediate structures [91]. These structures can evolve into macrostructures, forming R-CDs through carbonization and aromatization processes.

In addition, FTIR and XPS analysis showed that the R-CDs present silicon in their structure, suggesting that the silica from the rice husk was incorporated into the structure of the carbon dots. This result shows a process of self-doping, contributing to the structural and functional modification of the materials obtained. This indicates that 10–20 % of silica in rice husks contributes to CDs formation and doping. Previous works obtained silica-doped CDs using (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) [92], or tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) [93–95], while others employed complex routes involving multiple steps and corrosive acids, making the process more intricate [72]. However, this study demonstrates that silica incorporation into the R-CD structure is possible without additional silica sources, utilizing a green route with only rice husks.

Doping CDs with silica offers several advantages, including improved optical properties, thermal and chemical stability, dispersibility, lower toxicity, enhanced biocompatibility for biomedical applications, and increased sensitivity and selectivity for sensor applications [92,96]. This opens new opportunities to explore silica doping using only rice husks.

It, therefore, remains a challenge for the scientific community to unravel the formation of R-CDs from complex biomass and the exact mechanism of their luminescence. This work was a pioneering effort in the search for answers but at a preliminary stage. In addition, it was the first work to explore the formation of carbon dots by studying the degradation and conversion of biomass constituents according to kinetics, varying the reaction time considerably. In this way, it was possible to arrive at an optimized synthesis condition concerning the solids load, reaction temperature, and synthesis time to make production scalable.

Regarding lignocellulosic biomass, the direct conversion of constituents into R-CDs is laborious and requires more drastic reaction conditions. For this reason, obtaining a high degree of conversion remains a crucial concern. Furthermore, the mechanism proposed in this work is preliminary and does not consider the complex chemical interactions between the components in the reaction mixture. The complexity of these interactions represents a significant limitation to the precise identification of the processes involved. Future studies could address this limitation through the isolated investigation of monomers and the controlled addition of specific compounds, allowing for the validation and refinement of the mechanism suggested here.

Finally, using alternative green sources as precursors for forming CDs is highly encouraged, with numerous studies published in this area (Table S5). However, it remains essential to investigate the composition of these sources to assess the conversion's feasibility.

Atrazine is the second most widely used herbicide globally, found in surface and groundwater. Its exposure is associated with various risks to human health and ecosystem disturbances [97]. Because of this problem, developing efficient strategies for detecting this persistent agrochemical in the environment is essential. Based on the results presented, the CDs effectively fulfilled the criteria required for a fluorescent probe. Thus, the feasibility of applying R-CDs as atrazine sensors was explored.

The fluorometric response of R-CDs against different interfering compounds, including imidacloprid, glyphosate, mesotrione, dicamba, diuron, semicarbazone, and acephate, was evaluated by adding 500 µmol/L of each compound to the probe mixture, adjusted to the optimal pH for detection (Fig. S5, supplementary material) [50]. The R-CDs showed a high sensitivity for the detection of atrazine (Fig. 10 a).

In a subsequent analysis, the sensitivity and feasibility of the R-CDs-based sensor were examined in more depth. Detection was carried out by adding various concentrations of atrazine to an aqueous solution of R-CDs, and the fluorescence spectral profile was recorded in the absence and presence of this contaminant (Fig. 10b). It was noted that the R-CDs exhibited a characteristic emission peak at 445 nm in the absence of atrazine. In contrast, the gradual addition of atrazine resulted in a progressive decrease in fluorescence intensity, with no shift in the emission wavelength. The introduction of 20.00 µmol/L of atrazine caused a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs, confirming the sensor's selective detection capacity. The magnitude of the fluorescence extinction was directly proportional to the concentration of atrazine.

Based on the fluorescence data obtained, a calibration curve was constructed, which showed a linear relationship between the relative emission intensity and the atrazine concentrations in the range of 0.5 to 20.00 µmol/L, with a correlation coefficient of 0.989 (Fig. 10c). The fluorescence extinction efficiency was expressed by the equation (F0-F/F0) = 1.001C + 7.285, where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs in the presence and absence of atrazine [98].

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using equation Stern-Volmer (Eq.8) [98].
(8)
LOD =
3 σ
k
where σ is the standard deviation of the R-CD measurements in the blank condition (N = 10), and k is the slope of the calibration curve [51,98,99]. The LOD obtained was 2.91 µmol/L, demonstrating the sensor's high sensitivity for detecting atrazine. The sensor's performance was compared to other fluorescent sensors available in the literature (Table S6, Supplementary Material).

A new strategy was developed to provide mechanistic insights into forming R-CDs. The results from the biomass composition before and after hydrothermal synthesis, along with the characterization of R-CDs, offered valuable information for understanding the proposed study. Thus, the effects of solid loading and temperature on the degradation of biomass constituents and their conversion into R-CDs were elucidated. The reaction temperature and solid loading significantly influenced the efficient conversion of biomass into R-CDs. Elevated temperatures resulted in a lower cellulose fraction in the solid phase, decomposition of hemicellulose, and a high concentration of residual lignin and ash. Consequently, most glucan and xylan were solubilized into the liquid fraction. Hydrothermal synthesis leads to chemical transformations, converting polysaccharides into lower molecular weight molecules such as cellooligosaccharides and arabinoxylans. These molecules further convert into glucose, xylose, and arabinose, as well as hydroxymethylfurfural and furfural. These compounds may undergo further dehydration reactions, forming carbonaceous structures that will be converted into R-CDs. Additionally, reaction kinetics determined an optimized synthesis condition for R-CDs (220 °C − 3 % w/v), where efficient biomass conversion and high QY were achieved within a minimum reaction time of 4 h. This study provides a comprehensive analysis of how biomass composition affects R-CD properties. The research proved essential in improving the final product quality and optimizing R-CD synthesis, thereby encouraging the exploration of alternative biomass sources for CD synthesis and facilitating process scalability. However, additional studies with monomers/macromolecules from rice husks are still needed to strengthen the mechanism proposed in the present work. Additionally, due to the complexity of rice husk composition, the carbon dots obtained are expected to present heterogeneous features. For these reasons, future studies will focus on fractioning the final sample to characterize and assess the applications of each fraction. Other aspects that will be a focus in future studies are in-depth studies of quantum yield by applying relative (single point and slope method) and absolute approaches. Furthermore, due to prospective biomedical and sensing applications, in-depth toxicological studies will be an object of study in the next works.
